coredata.dc:title: Halloysite nanotubes supported Co2P bridged by carbon dots for enhanced hydrogen evolution from ammonia borane
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Yafei
Guo, Shu
Xue, Xin
Xiong, Chengli
Gao, Xiangyang
Zhang, Bing
coredata.dc:description: Rational design of efficient, low-cost and eco-friendly catalysts for hydrogen generation from solid-state hydrogen storage material ammonia borane (AB) is identified as an ideal approach for solving the problems of energy crisis and environmental pollution. Herein, we construct a robust and low-cost composite catalyst with the combination of non-noble metal cobalt phosphide (Co2P), carbon dots (CDs) and halloysite nanotubes (HNTs), in which CDs serve as the bridge and conductive mediator to link Co2P and HNTs for facilitating electron transfer. Attributing to the synergistic effects among Co2P, CDs and HNTs, the high conductivity of CDs and the dispersion/supporting of HNTs, the as-prepared Co2P-CDs/mHNTs composite catalyst displays a considerably high hydrogen evolution rate with TOF of 178.02 molH2 molco2P −1 min−1, which is much higher than Co2P/HNTs, Co2P/CDs and other non-noble metals-based catalysts. This work provides a new insight and bridging strategy for the design of low-cost composite catalysts with excellent performance toward AB hydrolysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Halloysite nanotubes
Carbon dots
Conductivity
Transition metal phosphates
Ammonia borane
Hydrogen generation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Modification of HNTs with amino groups
Synergistic effects of Co2P-CDs/mHNTs toward AB hydrolysis
Mechanism of AB hydrolysis catalyzed by Co2P-CDs/mHNTs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Special Project on Basic Research and Cultivation of Zhengzhou University
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Leading Talents of Scientific and Technological Innovation in Central China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U20041100 and U22A20143 ), Leading Talents of Scientific and Technological Innovation in Central China (224200510005), and Special Project on Basic Research and Cultivation of Zhengzhou University (Grant No. JC22257010 ). The authors really appreciate Modern Analysis and Gene Sequencing Center of Zhengzhou University in help of characterization.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns and (b) FTIR spectra of the samples.

(a) SEM (b) TEM images of mHNTs; (c) TEM image of CDs (Inset image is corresponding HR-TEM image); (d) Particle size distribution and UV absorption spectra of CDs (Inset is the fluorescence of CDs); TEM images of (e) CDs/mHNTs, (f) Co2P/mHNTs, (g and h) Co2P-CDs/mHNTs; (i and j) HR-TEM images of Co2P-CDs/mHNTs; (k) The corresponding EDS elemental mapping of Co2P-CDs/mHNTs.

(a) Survey XPS spectrum of the as-prepared Co2P-CDs/mHNTs; high-resolution XPS spectra of: (b) C 1 s in Co2P-CDs/mHNTs; (c) Co 2p in Co-CDs/mHNTs, Co2P-CDs/mHNTs and Co2P/mHNTs; (d) P 2p in Co2P-CDs/mHNTs and Co2P/mHNTs.

(a) Hydrolysis of AB catalyzed via various catalysts with different mass ratios of Co and CDs/mHNTs; (b) the corresponding TOF values; (c) hydrolysis of AB with different amounts of mHNTs-CDs/Co2P catalyst at 298 K; (d) Hydrolysis of AB with mHNTs-CDs/Co2P at different temperatures (Inset is the Arrhenius plot).
(a) Cyclic performance test of Co2P-CDs/mHNTs, (b) TEM, (c) HR-TEM, Co 2p and B 1 s XPS spectra of the Co2P-CDs/mHNTs after six cycles.
(a) Diagram of hydrolysis performance of AB by different catalysts. (b) The corresponding TOF values.
The mechanism illustration of AB hydrolysis on Co2P-CDs/mHNTs.

head.ce:title: Halloysite nanotubes supported Co2P bridged by carbon dots for enhanced hydrogen evolution from ammonia borane

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanics and Safety Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanics and Safety Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanics and Safety Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rational design of efficient, low-cost and eco-friendly catalysts for hydrogen generation from solid-state hydrogen storage material ammonia borane (AB) is identified as an ideal approach for solving the problems of energy crisis and environmental pollution. Herein, we construct a robust and low-cost composite catalyst with the combination of non-noble metal cobalt phosphide (Co2P), carbon dots (CDs) and halloysite nanotubes (HNTs), in which CDs serve as the bridge and conductive mediator to link Co2P and HNTs for facilitating electron transfer. Attributing to the synergistic effects among Co2P, CDs and HNTs, the high conductivity of CDs and the dispersion/supporting of HNTs, the as-prepared Co2P-CDs/mHNTs composite catalyst displays a considerably high hydrogen evolution rate with TOF of 178.02 molH2 molco2P −1 min−1, which is much higher than Co2P/HNTs, Co2P/CDs and other non-noble metals-based catalysts. This work provides a new insight and bridging strategy for the design of low-cost composite catalysts with excellent performance toward AB hydrolysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Considerable attention has been increasingly attracted in clean and renewable hydrogen energy with the gradual depletion of conventional fossil fuels energy and their associated severe environmental deterioration issues [1]. However, the safe and efficient storage and transport of hydrogen is still considered as a huge challenge, which urgently needs to be addressed due to the large volume, small density, inflammable and explosive properties of hydrogen [2]. Physical hydrogen storage (eg. high pressure compressing and low temperature liquefying) usually requires extra energy and specific cylinders, which undoubtedly brings problems of poor security and high expense. Comparatively, chemical hydrogen storage (eg. solid hydrogen storage compounds) is more prospective attributing to its higher safety, easier transportation, larger storage capacity, lower price and less energy consumption [2,3]. Ammonia borane (AB), as one of the frequently used solid hydrogen storage materials, has merits of high hydrogen capacity (19.6 wt%), non-toxicity, excellent water solubility, low molecular weight and mild hydrogen release conditions, making it identified as a competitive candidate of chemical hydrogen storage material [2–4]. Generally, hydrolysis will not take place with the absence of catalysts. In the presence of appropriate catalysts, one mole of AB can be completely hydrolyzed at room temperature to produce three moles of hydrogen (Eq. (1), and the hydrogen release rate is absolutely dependent on the utilized catalysts [3,5]. Therefore, great endeavors should be devoted to developing stable catalysts with high activity for achieving fast hydrogen release from AB.
(1)
NH
3
BH
3
+4H
2
O
→
catalyst
NH
4
+
B
OH
4
-
+3H
2

In the past few years, both noble and non-noble metals have been explored and investigated extensively and deeply in catalyzing AB hydrolysis. Undoubtedly, noble metals outperform non-noble metals in catalyzing dehydrogenation reactions [6]. However, precious metal catalysts are not a cost-effective option because cost and source are crucial considerations in industrial catalysis. By contrast, transition metals-based materials (eg. transition metal phosphides, transition metal nitrides and etc.) are abundant, available and inexpensive, thus have been increasingly surveyed in the field of catalysis [7–13]. Especially, transition metal phosphates as robust catalysts have been extensively studied due to their proper electron transfer from metal to P as well as their good stability [13–15]. However, the tendency towards aggregation and sintering of nanoparticles during phosphating process are unfavorable for the improvement of catalytic activity due to the decreased surface area and exposed active sites, resulting in the reduction of the catalytic activity of transition metal phosphates [15]. Dispersing transition metal phosphides on supporting materials (eg. carbon and graphene oxide) is an effective approach to solve the above-mentioned issues as well as reducing metal consumption [5,9,10]. Nonetheless, most of the supports are synthetic materials, involving in complicated manufacturing process, extra energy consumption and exorbitant price. As a consequence, it is desperately needed to seek an alternative approach to settle the above problems.

Halloysite nanotubes (HNTs, Al2Si2O5(OH)4·nH2O) are a kind of natural aluminosilicate nanotubular clay minerals with negative Si-OH outer surface and positive Al-OH inner surface, which are easily functionalized by –NH3, -SH, -PO3 and so on [16,17]. HNTs have been widely investigated as catalysts carrier due to their abundance, environmentally friendliness, availability, low cost, large surface area, tubular structure, and opposite and modifiable surface properties [17–20]. There is a fact that HNTs are likely to substitute the exorbitant synthetic materials as catalysts’ supports. Considering the unique structure and surface properties of HNTs, we have loaded Pt, AgPd and NiCoP catalysts on HNTs with different methods for AB hydrolysis, which achieves high stability and TOF of 321, 90 and 47.5 molH2 molcat −1 min−1, respectively, suggesting the practicability and feasibility of HNTs as catalyst supports [16,19,20]. Apparently, noble metals-based catalysts Pt/HNTs and AgPd/HNTs are proved to possess better catalytic activity than non-noble-based catalyst NiCoP/HNTs. However, the involving of noble metals undoubtedly increases overall expense of the catalysts. Decorating transition metal phosphides on HNTs has more extensive spreading values and prospects for practical application. However, HNTs have low intrinsic conductivity, which is unfavorable for electron transport and mass transfer of the catalysts in the catalytic reaction. To attain better catalytic activity of non-noble metals on HNTs, it is substantially required to introduce an intrinsic driving force on HNTs surface to facilitate electron transfer.

Carbon dots (CDs), a new rising star in the carbon family, have attracted considerable attention in catalysis, energy production, environmental remediation and sensors due to their high quantum yield, small size (0D, less than 10 nm), abundant functional groups, and high electron mobility [21–28]. Recently, CDs and metal coordination composites have been reported to catalyze hydrolysis of AB with boosted performance [23,24]. The reasons probably lie in that CDs can provide unblocked channel for electron transfer and abundant adsorption sites for AB and water molecules, resulting in a low energy barrier for activation of reaction and substantial improvement of the catalytic rate [24]. Thus, the outstanding electron transfer ability makes CDs fascinating supports for transitional metal catalyst. Besides, CDs with rich functional groups are more likely to interlink with metal ions such as Co2+, Cu2+, Ni2+ and etc. attributing to the strong complexing action between surface groups and metal ions, which not only creates a highly effective catalytic interface but also improves the electron transfer of the catalyst [23]. However, although CDs have the ability to improve the electron transfer of the catalysts, they usually tend to aggregate and collide because of the size limitation, which in turn goes against the enhancement of the catalytic activity.

Considering the merits and limitations of HNTs, CDs and transition metal phosphides, we anticipate that it is reasonable and feasible to integrate HNTs, CDs and transition metal together to obtain high-performance catalysts toward AB hydrolysis. The 0D morphology and small size of CDs makes them readily being loaded on the surface of HNTs. Herein, in this work, CDs are immobilized on –NH3 modified HNTs (mHNTs) surface (CDs/mHNTs) to provide electron transfer driving force as well as inhibit their aggregation, followed by decorating Co2P on CDs/mHNTs (Co2P-CDs/mHNTs), in which CDs serve as the bridge and conductive mediator to link Co2P and HNTs for facilitating electron transfer, thus making up for the shortage of HNTs and enhancing the catalytic activity of the catalyst. CDs are anchored on mHNTs and Co2P nanoparticles are inlaid on CDs/mHNTs, which prevents their respective aggregation, provides partial contact between CDs and Co2P and achieves the maximum exposure of active sites. As expected, benefiting from the high conductivity of CDs, the unique structure of HNTs and the interfacial interaction among Co2P, CDs and HNTs, the prepared Co2P-CDs/mHNTs displays superior catalytic activity and high stability toward AB hydrolysis, reaching a high turnover frequency (TOF) of 178.02 molH2 molco2P −1 min−1, which is higher than Co2P/HNTs, Co2P/CDs and most non-noble metals based catalysts. This work puts forward a creative approach for the rational design of high-performance catalysts for the hydrolysis of AB based on conductive bridge.

HNTs were obtained from Henan province (China), which were pretreated with 1 M of HCl to purify impurities. Poplar catkin was collected from different areas in Zhengzhou and rinsed with ethanol and deionized water alternately to remove impurities before use. Sodium borohydride monohydrate (NaBH4 ≥97 %, AR) was acquired from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Corporation. AB (NH3BH3, ≥97 %, CAS reagent) was supplied from Shanghai Yien Chemical Technology Co., LTD. 3-aminopropyl triethoxy silane (APTES, 99 %, AR), methylbenzene (C7H8, AR), N-(3-(dimethylamino)-propyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 99 %, AR), (N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98 %, AR), cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 99 %, AR) and sodium hypophosphite monohydrate (NaPO2H2·H2O, 99 %, AR) were purchased from Aladdin. Deionized water with a specific resistance of 18.25 MΩ·cm was used in all the experiments.

CDs were synthesized from poplar catkin by hydrothermal method. To be specific, 1 g of poplar catkin was added into 60 mL of deionized water with stirring for 2 h to disperse uniformly. Next, the mixture was poured into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave followed by being heated at 473 K for 8 h. After being cooled to room temperature, the extracted filtrate was filtered through a micron filter membrane (0.22 μm) to wipe off large particles, and then the filtration was dialyzed with a 500 Da dialysis bag for 24 h to filter out salts and small molecules in the solution to purify CDs. The mass concentration of CDs (2.56 mg/mL) was obtained by freeze-drying method, and the detailed process is provided in ESI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modification of HNTs with amino groups
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, 2 mL of APTES was dissolved into 25 mL of toluene with stirring for 30 min to form a homogenous solution. Then, 0.6 g of HNTs was added into the above solution with ultrasonic dispersion for 30 min. Next, the suspension was refluxed at 393 K for 24 h. Afterward, the solid was collected by centrifugation, washed with toluene and deionized water alternately for several times to remove the unreacted APTES, and finally dried at 343 K to obtain amino modified HNTs, which was denoted as mHNTs.

The immobilization of CDs on HNTs was adopted by the method of classic EDC/NHS reaction with the linking of –COOH and the –NH2 groups. Briefly, 30 mL of CDs solution (2.56 mg/mL) was dispersed into the mixture solution of EDC (15 mg) and NHS (18 mg) to activate carboxyl groups on CDs under magnetic stirring for 2 h. Then, mHNTs were added into the above solution and stirred for 4 h. Finally, the solid was collected by centrifugation, washed with deionized water and dried overnight in vacuum at 333 K, which was labelled as CDs/mHNTs.

Typically, 2 g of NaPO2H2·H2O was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water, and then 100 mg of CDs/mHNTs was added into the above solution with ultrasonic dispersion for 30 min to obtain a uniform suspension. Afterward, 0.3 mmol of Co(NO3)2·6H2O aqueous solution was added into the suspension dropwise with constant stirring. The above mixture was continued stirred for 12 h, followed by addition of excessive NaBH4. After continuous stirring for 30 min to guarantee the disappearance of all the bubbles, the resulted Co2P-CDs/mHNTs (the mass ratio of Co2P and CDs/mHNTs was 18:100) was collected by centrifugation, washed with water for several times and dried at 333 K in vacuum overnight. For comparison, Co2P/mHNTs and Co2P/CDs were also prepared following the above procedure by substitution of CDs/mHNTs with mHNTs and CDs, respectively. Besides, different Co2P-CDs/mHNTs samples with different mass ratio of Co2P and CDs/mHNTs were prepared following the same procedure except for changing the amount of Co(NO3)2·6H2O to 0.1, 0.2, 0.4 and 0.5 mmol, respectively (the mass ratio of Co and CDs/mHNTs were 6:100, 12:100, 24:100 and 30:100 respectively).

Scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi S2400) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Talosf200s, 200 kV) were employed to observe the morphology and structure of the synthesized samples. X-ray Diffraction (XRD) pattern was operated by a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer to determine the crystal structure of the samples. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured with a Thermo Scientific-Escalb 250XI electron spectrometer to analyze the surface property of the samples. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR 300, Thermo Nicolet) was utilized to investigate the functional groups on the samples. Raman spectra were recorded by using a Raman-atomic force microscope imaging analysis system (Lab RAMHR Evolution, HORIBA). The inductively couple plasma atomic emission spectrometer (ICP-AES) was adopted to measure the actual element content of the catalyst and the detailed process was provided in ESI.

The catalytic hydrogen production performance of the prepared catalysts was evaluated according to the following procedure. Specifically, a certain amount of the catalyst was added into 2 mL of deionized water with ultrasonic dispersion for 30 min to obtain a uniform suspension, and then the suspension was poured into a three-neck flask with one mouth connected with a burette and one mouth sealed. Next, 1 mL of AB solution was quickly injected into the above flask from the third opening mouth, which was quickly sealed to avoid the leakage of the generated hydrogen, because since then the catalytic hydrolysis of AB had immediately begun. The amount of hydrogen was recorded by the displacement of the water in the burette. The temperature of the system was maintained with a constant-temperature bath at 298 K. The recyclability experiments were performed using the same approach, except that the catalyst was separated after the first run and dispersed in a second batch of new matrix. The catalytic activity of the samples is evaluated by the turnover frequency (TOF) value, which is calculated according to the following Eq. (2), in which nH2 represents the mole number of generated H2, while n cat is the total mole number of active component (Co2P), t is the completed reaction time (min).
TOF = n H2/(n cat × t)(2)

To understand the synthesis mechanism of Co2P-CDs/mHNTs, the fabrication process is illustrated in Fig. 1 . HNTs possess abundant Si-OH groups on the surface. When HNTs and APTES are refluxed, the -Si-(OC2H5)3 groups in APTES react with Si-OH groups on the surface of HNTs and form -Si-O-Si groups, which results in the grafting of –NH2 groups on the surface of HNTs (derived from APTES, mHNTs). Afterward, with the help of EDC/NHS reaction, the –COOH groups on CDs can be activated, which react with the –NH2 groups on the mHNTs to accelerate the carboxyl-ammonia reaction, thus resulting in the immobilization of CDs on HNTs (CDs/HNTs). Subsequently, when Co2+ and NaH2PO4·H2O are added into CDs/HNTs suspension, Co2+ can be attached onto the negatively charged CDs surface by electrostatic adsorption, while PO2 3− ions are inclined to be electrostatically absorbed to positive Co2+. Finally, Co2P nanoparticles can be formed on the surface of CDs/mHNTs with the reduction of NaBH4 (Co2P-CDs/mHNTs).

The crystal structures of the samples were investigated by XRD (Fig. S1 and Fig. 2 a). Obviously, CDs show a broad peak at about 22.4° corresponding to crystalline carbon, indicating the successful formation of CDs from poplar catkin (Fig. S1) [29,30]]. For HNTs, the peaks located at 2θ of 12.02°, 20.07°, 25.6°, 35.02°, 62.68° and 73.79° can be indexed as the (001), (100), (002), (110), (300) and (220) planes of 7 Å dehydrated halloysite, respectively (Al2Si2O5(OH)4, JCPDS Card No. 29–1487). The impurity peaks of SiO2 can also be detected in HNTs, which agree well with the result in previous report [16]. After being modified with APTES, mHNTs show identical peaks in comparison with those of HNTs, indicating that the grafting of APTES onto HNTs does not alter the crystallinity of HNTs. However, all the peak intensities appear weaker, implying the successful grafting of APTES on HNTs surface. When CDs are loaded on mHNTs surface, except for the diffraction peaks of HNTs, no diffraction peak of CDs occurs, possibly on account of the low loading amount of CDs [31]. The XRD pattern of Co2P-CDs/mHNTs shows additional peaks at 40.7°, 43.3° and 44.1° compared with CDs/mHNTs, which correspond to the (121), (211) and (130) phases of Co2P (JCPDS Card No. 32–0306) [32], proving that Co2P nanoparticles are successfully immobilized on the CDs/mHNTs.

In order to investigate the functional groups of the samples, FTIR spectra were recorded as shown in Fig. 2b. For CDs, the FTIR spectrum shows the stretching vibrations of N-H (3473 cm−1), O-H (3018 cm−1), C-H (2622 cm−1), C = O (1691 cm−1), C-N (1389 cm−1), and C-O (1215 cm−1), implying the existence of polyaromatic structures and oxygen/nitrogen groups [31]. The rich functional groups on CDs afford active sites to coordinate Co2P, which is beneficial for the stability of the Co2P-CDs/mHNTs composite. For HNTs, the peaks at 3697 and 3627 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of hydroxyl groups, while the peak at 907 cm−1 is ascribed to the deformation vibration of hydroxyl groups. The abundant hydroxyl groups favor the functionalization of HNTs with APTES. The peak at 1012 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibrations of Si-O-Si. The 522 cm−1 peak is attributable to the deformation vibration of Al-O-Si. Co2P-CDs/mHNTs displays the stretching vibrations of O-H (2984 cm−1) and C-H (2900 cm−1), which negatively shift by 24 and 28 cm−1 respectively in comparison to CDs due to the effect of HNTs and Co2P. It is worth noting that the C = O groups of CDs and the stretching vibrations of hydroxyl groups of HNTs cannot be detected in Co2P-CDs/mHNTs, implying chemical reaction occurs between C = O and –OH when loading CDs on mHNTs. These results are in good agreement with the XRD analysis.

The micromorphologies of the synthesized catalysts were characterized by SEM and TEM (Fig. 3 ). As can be seen in Fig. 3a and 3b, mHNTs exhibit smooth surface and hollow tubular structure with average length of 500–2000 nm, outer and inner diameters of 50–100 nm and 20–50 nm, respectively, which is nearly the same as original HNTs (Fig. S2) and provides sufficient surface for loading of nanoparticles [33]. Fig. 3c reveals the uniform distribution of CDs with diameter of 1–10 nm and average diameter of 6 nm. Meanwhile, HR-TEM shows that CDs have a crystal interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm, which further confirms the successful preparation of CDs [31]. CDs were repeatedly synthesized using biomass poplar catkins from different sources as precursors, and TEM characterization and particle size statistical analysis were performed (Fig. S3). The results show that the CDs with an average diameter of 6 nm are uniformly dispersed, proving that the CDs synthesized by this method have good reproducibility. In addition, the CDs show strong blue fluorescence and also display UV absorption at 339 nm, which is typical n-π* transitions for conjugated C = O and C = N bonds (Fig. 3d) [29], further confirming the formation of CDs from poplar catkin. This result is consistent with the aforementioned XRD and FTIR analysis. After the EDC/NHS reaction, CDs are uniformly and firmly anchored on mHNTs (Fig. 3e). The presence of C, O, Al and Si element peaks was confirmed from energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) in Fig. S4, revealing the successful formation of CDs/mHNTs. For comparison, TEM testing of Co2P/mHNTs is also performed, and it is found that Co2P is uniformly decorated on the surface of mHNTs (Fig. 3f). For Co2P-CDs/mHNTs, HNTs still maintain a complete tubular structure during the synthesis of the catalyst (Fig. 3g). Co2P particles and CDs are evenly anchored on the surface of mHNTs due to the strong complexation effect of the functional groups on CDs and mHNTs for Co2+, which achieves maximum exposure of active sites and contributes to the formation of catalytic interfaces, thereby reducing the reaction barriers and accelerating AB hydrolysis. In addition, it is found that Co2P particles covers partial of CDs and there is bridge between CDs and Co2P through Fig. 3h and i, which is beneficial to the formation of an efficient catalytic interface. From the HR-TEM image of CDs-Co2P/mHNTs (Fig. 3j), the well-resolved lattice fringes of carbon (0.21 nm) and Co2P (0.22 nm) can be detected, confirming the successful anchoring of Co2P and CDs on HNTs. The composition of Co2P-CDs/mHNTs was further analyzed using EDS. From Fig. S5, the presence of C, O, Al, Si, P and Co element peaks is confirmed, which is consistent with the ICP-AES results (Table S1), further revealing the successful formation of Co2P-CDs/mHNTs. The corresponding atomic percentage (at %) and weight percentage (wt %) of the C, O, Al, Si, P and Co elements are shown in the inset of Fig. S5, where the atomic ratio of P and Co is close to 1:2. Fig. 3k reveals the element mapping image of EDS, demonstrating the uniform distribution of the C, O, Al, Si, P and Co elements in the Co2P-CDs/mHNTs. It is worth noting that the elements of P and Co signals are located outside while Al, Si and O signals are situated inside, indicating Co2P is on the outermost layer and HNTs is located at the core of the catalyst.

The chemical states and electronic interaction of CDs-Co2P/mHNTs were further analyzed by XPS. The survey spectrum in Fig. 4 a confirms the coexistence of Al, Si, O, C, N, Co and P in Co2P-CDs/mHNTs, which agrees well with the elemental mapping results in Fig. 3k. The corresponding high-resolution C 1 s (Fig. 4b) can be decomposed into three peaks at binding energies of 288.6, 286.2 and 284.5 eV, assigned to the C = O, C-O and C-C valence states of the carbon atom, respectively [30,34,35], indicating the existence of functional groups on the CDs, which contributes to the stable binding of CDs and mHNTs [35]. The high-resolution Co 2p spectrum of Co2P-CDs/mHNTs (Fig. 4c) contains two spin-orbit doublets and two shakeup satellites [8,9,13]. Specifically, the binding energies at 777.8 eV and 781.3 eV correspond to the Co 2p3/2, while the binding energies at 793.2 eV and 797.3 eV are assigned to Co 2p1/2, proving the co-existence of Co2+ and Co3+ with a ratio of 3.3:1 [13]. Meanwhile, two peaks at 786.2 and 803.1 eV are correspondent to the associated shakeup satellites [8,36]. It is worth noting that the binding energy of Co 2p3/2 peak in Co2P-CDs/mHNTs shows a negative shift of 0.46 eV compared with Co2P/mHNTs, which indicates the existence of strong electronic interaction and effective catalytic interface between Co2P and CDs [12,13]. The effect of P doping is insightful through Co 2p in Co-CDs/mHNTs. The binding energy of the Co 2p peak in Co2P-CDs/mHNTs is negatively shifted by 0.51 eV, attributing to the partial electron transfer from Co to P, which promotes the adsorption of H2O and the desorption of H2 [37,38]. The high positive charge on the Co can significantly increase the hydride-ion accepting sites, whereas the negatively charged P effectively captures protons owing to a strong electrostatic affinity, thus facilitating hydrogen generation from AB hydrolysis [37]. As displayed in Fig. 4d, the binding energy peak appearing at 137.2 eV can be assigned to P-O due to surface oxidation, and the other two peaks located at 129.8 and 128.9 eV can be ascribed to P 2p1/2 and P 2p3/2, respectively, further proving the construction of Co-P in the catalyst [29]. Interestingly, the P 2p of the Co2P/mHNTs peak shifts a higher binding energy of 0.5 eV, which further proves the existence of electron transfer and catalytic interface between Co2P and CDs [13,39]. Generally, the decrease in binding energy reveals an increase in electron concentration, leading to an enhanced electron shielding effect, thus enriching electronic states and optimizing the electronic structure configuration [40]. These results indicate that there are strong electronic interactions and tight heterogeneous interfaces between Co2P and CDs species, which accelerate the electron transfer efficiency and reduce the reaction activation energy, thereby enhancing the catalytic reaction rate [12,13].

The hydrolysis of AB by various catalysts with different mass ratios of Co and CDs/mHNTs was carefully evaluated under 298 K in a thermostat water bath. As displayed in Fig. 5 a, with the increase of the initial amount of Co metal, the catalytic hydrolysis rate of AB is accelerated. However, the TOF values of the catalysts present a volcanic curve trend (Fig. 5b) with the increase of Co metal, which increase gradually with the increase of the mass ratio of Co and CDs/HNTs from 6:100 to 18:100, reaching a plateau of 178.02 molH2 molCo2P −1 min−1. This value is much higher than previous reported non-noble metal-based catalysts (Table S2). The reason for the growing activity is mainly attributed to the increased active sites with the increase of Co metal. However, the TOF values gradually decrease with the continuous increase of Co metal, which may be because too much initial Co metal addition leads to agglomeration of Co nanoparticles during synthesis, resulting in a reduction of active sites. From the perspective of maximizing the performance of the catalyst and economizing on metal consumption, we employed the Co2P-CDs/mHNTs catalyst with Co and CDs/mHNTs mass ratio of 18:100 to catalytically hydrolyze AB for hydrogen generation in the subsequent experiments. Besides, with the increase of time, the hydrogen generation amount increases but the catalytic rate decreases for all the samples, which can be resulted from the decreased concentration of AB and the blockage of the active sites by by-product metaborate ions. It is noted that the actual amounts of Co loaded on CDs/mHNTs for all the samples are all only a little smaller than the theoretic amount measured by ICP-AES (Table S1), suggesting that most of the Co precursor can be anchored on the surface of CDs/mHNTs during catalysts preparation.

The influence of the amount of the Co2P-CDs/mHNTs catalyst in AB hydrolysis was further revealed. As shown in Fig. 5c, the hydrolysis reaction rate accelerates with the amount of Co2P-CDs/mHNTs increases from 5 to 25 mg, which is resulted from the increased active sites of the catalysts. We adopted 10 mg in the following experiments in this work for considering the balance between catalyst cost and performance. Besides, the hydrogen generation amount increases as the catalytic reaction proceeds. Fig. S6 shows the plot of ln[cat] against lnk, and the slope is calculated to be 1.07 after linear fitting, demonstrating that the reaction confirms to first order kinetics with respect to catalyst amount.

Temperature is also an important factor influencing the catalysts’ hydrogen generation rate. To study the effect of temperature on AB hydrolysis and obtain the apparent activation energy (Ea, the minimum energy required for the proceeding of a chemical reaction) of the catalytic reaction, hydrolysis production was also investigated from 298 K to 318 K (Fig. 5d). It is obvious that the hydrolysis rate increases with the increase of reaction temperature, indicating AB hydrolysis reaction is endothermic. The catalytic reaction can be divided into three stages. Initially, the catalyst has an activation process at the beginning of the reaction, resulting in a relatively slow catalytic reaction at this stage. As the reaction proceeds, the catalyst can fully participate in its catalytic reaction, which results in a rapid hydrogen evolution rate. During the final stage, the concentration of AB is very low and metaborate ions are accumulated on the surface of the catalyst, thereby causing a decrease of the catalytic reaction. Similar phenomena can also be found in previously reported literature [41]. According to the linear fitting of 1/T vs. lnk and Arrhenius equation, the Ea value is calculated to be 44.89 kJ mol−1, which is similar with those of most non-noble metal catalyst as shown in Table S2, indicating that Co2P-CDs/mHNTs has considerable potential for AB catalytic hydrolysis to produce hydrogen.

The recyclability of Co2P-CDs/mHNTs was examined considering its importance for practical applications (Fig. 6 a). It is shown that the catalytic activity of Co2P-CDs/mHNTs decreases with the increase of the number of cycles, but still exhibits good catalytic performance after six cycles. To determine the exact reasons for the slight decrease of catalytic activity, the Co2P-CDs/mHNTs after six cycles were characterized, including TEM, HR-TEM and XPS (Co and B narrow scan). It can be found that the morphology of the catalyst does not change significantly after cycling (Fig. 6b,) and CDs and Co2P can be evenly dispersed on mHNTs (Fig. 6c), which is beneficial to extending the life of the catalyst. It has been confirmed that metaborate ions are inevitable by-products in the hydrogen production process and have strong electrophilic ability, which may be deposited on the surface of Co2P-CDs/mHNTs during the reaction, thereby blocking the active sites of the catalysts [42–44]. It is found from Fig. 6b that the binding energy of the Co 2p peak of Co2P-CDs/mHNTs negatively shifts after cycling, indicating that metaborate ions form oxidizing environment on the Co surface and the corresponding electronic state density decreases, causing the blockage of the active sites and slight decrease of the catalyst’s recycling ability. Furtherly, we performed a narrow scan of boron for the recycled catalyst. The result shows that boron element exists in the catalyst after recycling and the boron 1 s peak is located at 192 eV, proving the generation of metaborate [44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synergistic effects of Co2P-CDs/mHNTs toward AB hydrolysis
ce:section.ce:para: In order to understand the unique performance of Co2P-CDs/mHNTs and the synergistic effects among Co2P, CDs and mHNTs for AB hydrolysis, control experiments were conducted as demonstrated in Fig. 7 . It is obvious that no hydrogen is produced with adding of CDs, HNTs, CDs/HNTs and CDs/mHNTs, indicating that the catalytic active sites are located on Co2P [32]. Pure Co2P shows a lower hydrogen production rate (TOF = 26.24 molH2 molCo2P −1 min−1), while Co2P/HNTs and Co2P/mHNTs reveal enhanced hydrogen production rates, with TOF values of 45.02 and 46.02 molH2 molCo2P −1 min−1, respectively. This is mainly due to the fact that HNTs serve as a good support to prevent the agglomeration of the active component Co2P and maximize the exposure of the active sites. Meanwhile, the TOF value of Co2P/CDs is 38.38 molH2 molCo2P −1 min−1, whereas Co2P-CDs/HNTs reveals increased TOF of 76.24 molH2 molCo2P −1 min−1, suggesting the high synergistic effect among Co2P, CDs and mHNTs facilitates the AB catalytic hydrolysis reaction, in which CDs can create an efficient catalytic interface with Co2P, thereby improving electron transmission efficiency, and HNTs as support can prevent the aggregation of CDs and Co2P. Encouragingly, Co2P-CDs/mHNTs exhibits faster rate of hydrogen production (TOF 178.02 molH2 molCo2P −1 min−1) than Co2P-CDs/HNTs. The reason may be due to the fact that the CDs are not effectively bonded to HNTs since the surface of both HNTs and CDs are negatively charged, which is unfavorable for the combination of CDs and HNTs. However, when HNTs are modified by APTES, the –NH2 groups can be grafted on the surface of HNTs (mHNTs), which are prone to bond with the –COOH groups activated by EDC/NHS reaction on CDs, thus facilitating the anchoring and dispersing of CDs on mHNTs. On the other hand, the interaction between CDs and Co2P can improve the activity of the catalyst because CDs can enhance the electron interaction between metal active components and promote the electron transfer in the catalytic process. CDs can provide a smooth channel for electron transfer and provide abundant adsorption sites for AB and water molecules, resulting in a lower energy barrier for reaction activation and thus greatly increasing the catalytic rate [24]. To confirm the effect of P doping, the synthesized Co-CDs/mHNTs is also tested for hydrogen production from AB hydrolysis, as shown in Figure S7. The results show that the performance of Co2P-CDs/mHNTs is better than that of Co-CDs/mHNTs, proving that P doping improves the catalytic performance. This is mainly due to the surface charge imbalance caused by the electronegativity difference between metals Co and P, thereby promoting charge transfer from Co to P [37,38]. Meanwhile, there are theoretical calculations that prove that P-doping can cause surface charge density difference changes and lower Bader charges, leading to a reduction in the d-band center [42,45]. In addition, the highly positively charged and negatively charged P on Co have excellent hydrogen ion-accepting sites and proton capturing capabilities respectively, thereby promoting the hydrolysis of AB to produce hydrogen [37]. Collectively, the synergistic effect between scaffold mHNTs, highly conductive CDs, highly active Co2P, and efficient catalytic interface significantly improves the catalytic performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of AB hydrolysis catalyzed by Co2P-CDs/mHNTs
ce:section.ce:para: The isotope effect experiment is a powerful tool to elucidate rate-determining step in different reactions, which is of great significance for understanding the mechanism of AB hydrolysis [4,46–48]. The isotopic kinetic experiment was performed using Co2P-CDs/mHNTs catalyst with D2O instead of H2O (Figure S8). The results show that the reaction rate of AB hydrolysis in H2O is much higher than the reaction rate in D2O, indicating that the kinetic isotope effect (KIE) is significant with the KIE value of 3.9 H2O has the same bonds as D2O except for the O-H bond, indicating that the cleavage of the O-H bond is the rate-determining step in AB hydrolysis [46,47].

Based on our experiments, the following possible mechanism for the hydrolysis of AB on Co2P-CDs/mHNTs is proposed as shown in Fig. 8 . The high surface area and unique structure of HNTs enable the CDs and Co2P active sites to be uniformly distributed on their surface, the small size of Co2P maximizes its activity and utilization, and the synergistic effects of CDs, Co2P and HNTs are conducive to enhancing the performance of the Co2P-CDs/mHNTs. The bridge of HNTs and Co2P by CDs can not only prevent the aggregation of active sites, but also facilitate the electrons and mass transfer, leading to strong adsorption of AB, which effectively excites the B-H bond in AB and reduces the reaction energy barrier [8]. Then, –OH bond in water attacks one of the B-H bonds in AB, resulting in the cleavage of B-H. The –OH from water combines with the remaining NH3BH2- to form NH3BH2OH, while the H* from water combine with the H from B-H to release one H2 molecule [39]. Following the same reaction mechanism, under the attacking of another two H2O molecules, the other two B-H bonds in NH3BH2OH break in sequence with forming of NH3B(OH)3 and releasing of two molecules of H2. Finally, NH3B(OH)3 continues reacting with one H2O molecule, obtaining NH4 + and B(OH)4 −. It is noted that the main mechanism for the H2 generation is the breaking of B-H and combination of H from O-H (derived from water) and B-H [6,40,49].

In summary, we successfully developed a robust and low-cost composite catalyst Co2P-CDs/mHNTs for AB dehydrogenation. The as-synthesized Co2P-CDs/mHNTs exhibits an excellent catalytic activity for AB dehydrogenation with TOF of 178.02 molH2 molCo2P −1 min−1, which is higher than most transition metal catalyst. The enhanced catalytic activity of Co2P-CDs/mHNTs can be attributed to the synergistic effects among Co2P, CDs and mHNTs. Specifically, (i) mHNTs serve as good support to effectively load CDs and Co2P, which achieves high exposure of adsorption and catalytic active sites; (ii) CDs not only provide unblocked channels for electron transfer, but also provide abundant adsorption sites for AB and water molecules; (iii) CDs can create an efficient catalytic interface with Co2P, thereby improving electron transmission efficiency and reducing reaction barriers, and greatly improving catalytic efficiency. This work sheds light on the bridging strategy for the rational design of efficient and low-cost composite catalysts towards AB hydrolysis to produce clean renewable hydrogen energy.

Yafei Zhao: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Shu Guo: Formal analysis. Xin Xue: Formal analysis. Chengli Xiong: Formal analysis. Xiangyang Gao: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization. Bing Zhang: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

The authors really appreciate Modern Analysis and Gene Sequencing Center of Zhengzhou University in help of characterization.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.149332.

ce:section.ce:para:
